evals.name: telecom-cancellation-process-reuse
evals.prompt: We built a customer service agent for our telecom company 6 months ago. It handles billing disputes, plan changes, and cancellations. Every time it encounters a new carrier's cancellation process, it rediscovers the steps from scratch — burning 15 minutes and 40+ LLM calls. How do we make it stop reinventing the wheel?
evals.expected_output: Recommends externalizing the discovered process into a persistent, retrievable resource rather than relying on context alone; names a concrete mechanism (strategy summary and/or workflow recording); addresses reuse on future similar tasks; surfaces a transferability or verification consideration.
expectations: Recommends externalizing the discovered process into a persistent, retrievable store (not just adding more context or a bigger window)
Names a specific mechanism: strategy summary (reflect on successful trajectory) or workflow recording/replay
Addresses how the stored experience is retrieved and reused on the next similar task
Surfaces a transferability gate (not every one-off gets stored) or a verification gate before reuse

evals.prompt: I'm designing an agent that fills out web forms for our sales team — same forms every day, just different data (different client names, different amounts). Right now it uses a full multimodal LLM every time to identify the fields. This is expensive and slow. Is there a way to record the first successful run and replay it?

our coding agent keeps making the same mistake: it tries to run tests before installing dependencies. it's failed on this 3 times this week across different repos. how do we make sure it doesn't repeat this failure pattern in future sessions?

evals.name: research-agent-learning-loop
evals.prompt: We're building a general-purpose research agent that should get better at finding information over time. After each successful research task, we want it to learn what worked — which search strategies, which sources, which synthesis approaches. How do we design this learning loop?
evals.expected_output: Describes an explicit distill→organize→apply→evolve learning loop; recommends strategy summaries (reflect on successful trajectory → structured note → retrieve for future tasks); includes a transferability gate; addresses how past experience is injected into new tasks.
expectations: Describes an explicit learning loop (distill → organize → apply → evolve, or equivalent) rather than implying the model learns automatically
Recommends strategy summaries: reflect on the successful trajectory and produce a structured, reusable note
Includes a transferability gate — not every trajectory becomes stored experience
Addresses retrieval and injection of prior experience into new tasks (semantic search over the experience store)

evals.name: over-escalation-prompt-sharpening
evals.prompt: My customer support agent keeps over-escalating to human agents — it escalates whenever a customer mentions a policy dispute, even when it could just explain the policy. The transfer rule in the system prompt is too vague. How do I systematically sharpen these behavioral rules from real failures without manually rewriting the prompt every time?
evals.expected_output: Identifies system prompt optimization / 'system prompt learning' as the mechanism; describes edge-case sharpening of the gray-zone rule; describes an automated loop (coding-agent-writes-diff + human review, and/or DSPy/OPRO/GEPA); distinguishes online case-by-case from offline batch.
expectations: Identifies system prompt optimization / 'system prompt learning' as the mechanism (not fine-tuning)
Frames the fix as sharpening rule boundaries through edge cases (the gray zone)
Describes an automated update loop: a coding agent reads the prompt and generates a reviewed diff, and/or an automated framework (DSPy/OPRO/GEPA)
Distinguishes online case-by-case optimization from offline batch optimization

evals.prompt: We're building an agent that automates financial data analysis. Sometimes it needs to pull data from sources we haven't pre-integrated — a new exchange, a new data provider. We want the agent to be able to find the right library, wrap it, and add it to its own tool set. How do we architect this?

How does my agent get better over time? Right now every session starts from zero — it doesn't remember what worked last week, doesn't know our internal conventions, doesn't have any of the domain knowledge it's accumulated. What's the right architecture for cross-session improvement without retraining?

evals.name: voyager-enterprise-tool-safety
evals.prompt: We want to implement something like Voyager for our enterprise automation agent — it should be able to discover new capabilities by searching our internal tool registry and GitHub, test them, and add them to its library. What are the safety guardrails we need to put around this?
evals.expected_output: Describes the tool-creation cycle (explore → verify task completion → store → reuse); emphasizes concrete safety boundaries (sandbox, security scan, whitelist, human review, pre-storage verification); addresses privilege escalation via tool composition; notes enterprise adaptation beyond the game setting.
expectations: Describes the tool-creation cycle: explore/search → verify task completion → store in library → reuse
Lists concrete safety boundaries: sandboxed execution, security scan of created tools, whitelist of permitted tool types, periodic human review
Addresses privilege escalation through tool composition (a composed tool inherits the union of component permissions)
Includes a pre-storage verification gate (the tool actually accomplishes the task before entering the library)

evals.prompt: I'm designing a skill generation pipeline for our domain-specific agent. When the agent encounters a new process it's never seen before, it should be able to generate a SKILL.md for that process and add it to its skill library. What's the right design for this pipeline?

We're using DSPy to optimize our agent's prompts. It's working well for initial prompt construction but we're not sure how to handle continuous evolution in production as new edge cases arrive. Should we keep running DSPy or switch to a different approach for ongoing improvement?

I want my agent to remember that I prefer concise responses, that I work in Python, and that I'm on the platform team at a fintech company. How do I store and retrieve these preferences across sessions?

We're designing the tool schema for our agent's database access tool. Should the `query` parameter accept raw SQL or a structured query object? How do we write the description so the model uses it correctly?

How should I structure my system prompt for a multi-step coding agent? I have persona, constraints, tool usage policy, and output format sections. What order should they go in and how do I keep the KV cache from getting invalidated every turn?

We want to fine-tune our customer service agent on 10,000 labeled examples of good conversations. Should we use SFT or RL? We have a working prompt-engineered version at 78% accuracy and want to push to 90%.

I'm building a coding agent that generates Python code. What patterns should I use for the code generation step — should it generate the full file at once or edit incrementally? How do I handle the case where the generated code doesn't compile?

our agent needs to store conversation summaries so it can reference past interactions. we're debating between a vector database and a simple key-value store. what's the right storage architecture for this?

I need to design the MCP tool interface for our internal search API. It has three endpoints: full-text search, semantic search, and filtered search. Should these be three separate tools or one unified tool with a `search_type` parameter?
We're setting up a GRPO training run for our agent. What's the right group size, KL coefficient, and reward normalization strategy? We have a code execution environment for the reward signal.

My agent's context window is filling up on long research tasks. I'm at 128k tokens and hitting the limit. Should I use sliding window compression, summarization, or a different approach to manage context growth?

evals.name: reusable-utility-functions-evolution
evals.prompt: We have a coding agent that writes and executes Python. I want it to generate reusable utility functions that it can call in future tasks. Is this agent self-evolution or just normal code generation?
evals.expected_output: Correctly identifies this as self-evolution when the generated functions are stored and reused across tasks (the distinguishing criterion is persistence + reuse, not one-off code gen); connects to tool creation / three-layer accumulation; routes one-off code-generation patterns to coding-agent-design.
expectations: Correctly identifies this AS self-evolution when functions are stored and reused across sessions (not one-off generation)
Names the distinguishing criterion: persistence + cross-task reuse, plus a verification gate before a function enters the reusable library
Connects to tool creation and three-layer capability accumulation (tools become building blocks)
Routes the mechanics of generating/editing the code itself to coding-agent-design
